name: Oral Candidiasis (Thrush)

key_questions: Inhaled corticosteroid use (most common cause — rinsing mouth after?)
Infant (<4 months) or breastfeeding mother-infant pair (treat both)?
red_flags: Unresponsive to antifungal → resistant Candida or alternative diagnosis
Infant <4 months with oral thrush — refer to physician
Breastfeeding mother-infant dyad — treat both (infant oral nystatin AND maternal nipples with miconazole or clotrimazole cream) to prevent ping-pong reinfection
ddx: Erythematous (denture stomatitis) — red, flat patches under dentures

first_line.name: Nystatin Suspension
first_line.drugs: Nystatin 100,000 units/mL: 4–6 mL swish and swallow QID × 7–14 days. Infant dosing: 1 mL each side of mouth (total 2 mL) QID × 7–14 days
first_line.notes: Topical antifungal — swish vigorously throughout mouth for 2 min, then swallow. Effective and cheap. Continue 48h after symptoms resolve. Works by binding ergosterol in fungal cell membrane. ONTARIO MA SCOPE: Nystatin IS prescribable by pharmacists under the OCP Minor Ailments algorithm for oral thrush. This is the practical first-line topical option in Ontario.
first_line.preferred_when: Mild-moderate thrush; first-line for most patients including infants and breastfeeding dyads

first_line.name: Clotrimazole Troches
first_line.drugs: Clotrimazole 10 mg lozenge: dissolve slowly in mouth 5× daily × 14 days
first_line.notes: Good compliance. Dissolve slowly — do not chew. More convenient than nystatin for some patients. NOTE: Clotrimazole 10 mg troches are NOT consistently available in Canadian pharmacies (often special order or unavailable). In most Ontario practice settings, nystatin suspension is the only reliable topical option. Confirm availability before recommending.

second_line.name: Fluconazole — systemic (NOT in OCP MA algorithm — requires physician referral)
second_line.drugs: Fluconazole 200 mg PO loading dose on Day 1, then 100 mg OD × 7–14 days (total course 7–14 days). Alternatively: 100–200 mg OD × 7–14 days without loading dose for mild-moderate cases
second_line.notes: CRITICAL DOSE NOTE: The correct dose for ORAL CANDIDIASIS is 100–200 mg daily × 7–14 days with a 200 mg loading dose. A single 150 mg dose is the regimen for VAGINAL candidiasis — this is the WRONG indication and dose for oral thrush. For moderate-severe or refractory oral candidiasis, or denture-associated thrush, or when topical agents fail. Check drug interactions (many CYP3A4 interactions — warfarin, calcineurin inhibitors, statins, oral hypoglycemics). ONTARIO MA SCOPE: Fluconazole (systemic) is NOT in the OCP Minor Ailments prescribing algorithm for oral thrush. Pharmacist must refer to physician for systemic antifungal.

non_pharm: ICS users: ALWAYS rinse mouth and gargle with water after each puffer use — prevents recurrence
Denture hygiene: remove dentures at night; soak in chlorhexidine 0.12% rinse or nystatin suspension to eliminate Candida biofilm from denture surface
Good oral hygiene: soft toothbrush twice daily
Address predisposing factors (diabetes control, dry mouth treatment)
Spacer device for ICS — reduces oropharyngeal deposition significantly
Infant: apply nystatin 1 mL to each side of mouth QID; ensure pacifiers and bottle nipples are sterilized daily
Breastfeeding dyad: apply miconazole 2% or clotrimazole 1% cream to maternal nipples after each feed (wipe off before next feed); treat infant orally simultaneously
refer_when: Odynophagia or dysphagia (esophageal involvement — requires systemic treatment and physician assessment)
Unresponsive to topical nystatin after 7–14 days
Immunocompromised patients (HIV, chemotherapy, high-dose systemic steroids) — systemic risk
Recurrent thrush without clear predisposing cause (may indicate undiagnosed diabetes, HIV, or immunodeficiency)
Requires systemic fluconazole (outside OCP MA scope — physician referral needed)

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Oral Candidiasis. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
RxTx (Canadian Pharmacists Association). Oral Candidiasis — Therapeutic Choices, 8th ed. (2023).
Pappas PG, et al. Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Candidiasis: 2016 Update by IDSA. Clin Infect Dis. 2016;62(4):e1–e50.
NICE. Candida — oral: Clinical Knowledge Summary (CKS). National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2023).
